items.passage: Solid-state batteries are energy-storage devices that replace the flammable liquid electrolyte of conventional lithium-ion cells with a solid material. This single substitution addresses a stubborn safety problem. [■] In a standard battery, the liquid carries charged particles between the two electrodes, but it can leak or ignite when overheated. A solid electrolyte, typically a ceramic or polymer, conducts ions while remaining stable at high temperatures. [■] Because the solid layer is rigid, it can also block the thin metallic spikes, called dendrites, that gradually pierce liquid cells and cause short circuits. [■] This durability allows engineers to pair the solid electrolyte with a pure lithium-metal anode, which stores far more energy than the graphite used today. The result is a cell that is both safer and lighter. [■] Yet manufacturing remains difficult: the solid layers must contact the electrodes perfectly, and even microscopic gaps raise resistance and reduce performance. Despite intensive research, producing these batteries cheaply at large scale continues to challenge the industry.
paragraphs: Solid-state batteries are energy-storage devices that replace the flammable liquid electrolyte of conventional lithium-ion cells with a solid material. This single substitution addresses a stubborn safety problem. [■] In a standard battery, the liquid carries charged particles between the two electrodes, but it can leak or ignite when overheated. A solid electrolyte, typically a ceramic or polymer, conducts ions while remaining stable at high temperatures.

[■] Because the solid layer is rigid, it can also block the thin metallic spikes, called dendrites, that gradually pierce liquid cells and cause short circuits. [■] This durability allows engineers to pair the solid electrolyte with a pure lithium-metal anode, which stores far more energy than the graphite used today. The result is a cell that is both safer and lighter.

[■] Yet manufacturing remains difficult: the solid layers must contact the electrodes perfectly, and even microscopic gaps raise resistance and reduce performance. Despite intensive research, producing these batteries cheaply at large scale continues to challenge the industry.

questions.stem: The word "stable" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: common
options.B: expensive
options.C: transparent
options.D: steady

questions.explanation: The solid electrolyte "remaining stable at high temperatures" means it stays steady or unchanged, unlike a liquid that ignites when overheated.

questions.stem: According to the passage, what allows engineers to use a pure lithium-metal anode?

options.A: The lower cost of ceramic materials
options.B: The high conductivity of graphite
options.C: The durability of the solid electrolyte layer
options.D: The leakage of the liquid electrolyte

questions.explanation: The passage states "This durability allows engineers to pair the solid electrolyte with a pure lithium-metal anode," referring to the solid layer's ability to block dendrites.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about graphite anodes?

options.A: They are more stable at high temperatures than lithium metal.
options.B: They cannot be used in conventional lithium-ion cells.
options.C: They prevent dendrites from forming inside the cell.
options.D: They hold less energy than lithium-metal anodes.

questions.explanation: Since the lithium-metal anode "stores far more energy than the graphite used today," graphite must store comparatively less energy.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention dendrites in paragraph 2?

options.A: To argue that liquid cells are cheaper to produce
options.B: To explain an additional advantage of the rigid solid layer
options.C: To describe how graphite anodes are manufactured
options.D: To show why high temperatures damage all batteries

questions.explanation: Dendrites are introduced to show that the rigid solid layer "can also block" these damaging spikes, illustrating a further benefit of the solid electrolyte.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'And because it does not flow, the solid electrolyte cannot leak out of a cracked casing the way a liquid can.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Location 1
options.B: Location 2
options.C: Location 3
options.D: Location 4

questions.explanation: The removed sentence elaborates on the "stubborn safety problem" of leaking, which is introduced just before slot 1 and before the detailed discussion of liquids leaking.

items.passage: Calligraphy is the artistic practice of forming written characters with deliberate beauty and controlled gesture. In the East Asian tradition, it has long been ranked among the highest visual arts, equal to painting. [■] A finished work is judged not by what the words say but by the quality of each stroke. The brush, loaded with ink, records the slightest change in pressure, speed, and angle, so a single line preserves the movement of the hand that made it. [■] Because the absorbent paper allows no correction, the artist must compose the entire piece in one continuous, unhesitating motion. [■] This demand for spontaneity means that years of disciplined practice precede a work that looks effortless. Critics therefore read a scroll almost like a recording of the writer's breathing and mood. [■] The same characters, written by two trained hands, can convey calm restraint or restless energy. Yet such expressiveness resists translation: a viewer unfamiliar with the script may admire the rhythm of the lines while missing the meanings that guided them, and even experts may disagree about a master's intent.
paragraphs: Calligraphy is the artistic practice of forming written characters with deliberate beauty and controlled gesture. In the East Asian tradition, it has long been ranked among the highest visual arts, equal to painting. [■] A finished work is judged not by what the words say but by the quality of each stroke. The brush, loaded with ink, records the slightest change in pressure, speed, and angle, so a single line preserves the movement of the hand that made it.

[■] Because the absorbent paper allows no correction, the artist must compose the entire piece in one continuous, unhesitating motion. [■] This demand for spontaneity means that years of disciplined practice precede a work that looks effortless. Critics therefore read a scroll almost like a recording of the writer's breathing and mood.

[■] The same characters, written by two trained hands, can convey calm restraint or restless energy. Yet such expressiveness resists translation: a viewer unfamiliar with the script may admire the rhythm of the lines while missing the meanings that guided them, and even experts may disagree about a master's intent.

questions.stem: The word "preserves" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: protects
options.B: shortens
options.C: records
options.D: decorates

questions.explanation: A line that "preserves the movement of the hand" keeps a record of that motion, so "records" fits the context.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, why must the artist work in one continuous motion?

options.A: The ink dries before the brush can be reloaded.
options.B: The characters must be written faster than in painting.
options.C: Critics prefer scrolls that are completed quickly.
options.D: The absorbent paper does not allow corrections.

questions.explanation: The passage states "Because the absorbent paper allows no correction, the artist must compose the entire piece in one continuous, unhesitating motion."

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true of calligraphy EXCEPT

options.A: It is ranked among the highest visual arts in East Asia.
options.B: A work is judged mainly by the meaning of its words.
options.C: The brush records changes in pressure and speed.
options.D: Years of practice precede an effortless-looking work.

questions.explanation: The passage states a work "is judged not by what the words say but by the quality of each stroke," so judging by meaning is the unsupported statement.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about a viewer unfamiliar with the script?

options.A: Such a viewer can appreciate the visual rhythm without grasping the meaning.
options.B: Such a viewer values restraint over energy in every work.
options.C: Such a viewer agrees easily with experts about the master's intent.
options.D: Such a viewer prefers painting to calligraphy.

questions.explanation: The passage says an unfamiliar viewer "may admire the rhythm of the lines while missing the meanings," implying appreciation of form without comprehension of meaning.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'For example, a rapid, sweeping line can suggest urgency, while a slow, even one suggests serenity.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Location 1
options.B: Location 2
options.C: Location 3
options.D: Location 4

questions.explanation: The example of fast and slow lines elaborates on how a single line "preserves the movement of the hand," fitting after that point at slot 2 and before the discussion of correction.

items.passage: Self-healing polymers are materials engineered to repair their own cracks without external intervention. Ordinary plastics pose a persistent problem: once a microscopic crack forms, stress concentrates at its tip and the fracture spreads until the part fails. [■] Such damage is usually invisible and impossible to mend, so components are discarded long before their bulk material is exhausted. Replacing them wastes energy and raw material. [■] Chemists answer this challenge by building repair into the molecular structure itself. One approach embeds tiny capsules of liquid healing agent throughout the polymer; when a crack tears the capsules open, the agent flows into the gap and hardens. [■] A second approach uses reversible bonds that break under stress but reform when the broken surfaces are pressed together or gently warmed. Both methods restore much of the original strength. [■] Yet the repair is rarely complete. Capsule systems can heal a given spot only once, because the reservoir there is spent, and reversible-bond materials are often too soft for heavy structural loads. For demanding applications, durable self-repair remains an unresolved goal.
paragraphs: Self-healing polymers are materials engineered to repair their own cracks without external intervention. Ordinary plastics pose a persistent problem: once a microscopic crack forms, stress concentrates at its tip and the fracture spreads until the part fails. [■] Such damage is usually invisible and impossible to mend, so components are discarded long before their bulk material is exhausted. Replacing them wastes energy and raw material.

[■] Chemists answer this challenge by building repair into the molecular structure itself. One approach embeds tiny capsules of liquid healing agent throughout the polymer; when a crack tears the capsules open, the agent flows into the gap and hardens. [■] A second approach uses reversible bonds that break under stress but reform when the broken surfaces are pressed together or gently warmed. Both methods restore much of the original strength.

[■] Yet the repair is rarely complete. Capsule systems can heal a given spot only once, because the reservoir there is spent, and reversible-bond materials are often too soft for heavy structural loads. For demanding applications, durable self-repair remains an unresolved goal.

questions.stem: The word "persistent" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: sudden
options.B: harmless
options.C: continuing
options.D: obvious

questions.explanation: A "persistent problem" is one that continues over time without going away, so "continuing" fits.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, how does the capsule approach repair a crack?

options.A: It warms the broken surfaces until they reform bonds.
options.B: A crack opens the capsules and releases an agent that hardens in the gap.
options.C: It thickens the polymer so cracks cannot start.
options.D: It presses the fractured surfaces together by hand.

questions.explanation: The passage states that "when a crack tears the capsules open, the agent flows into the gap and hardens."

questions.stem: The word "there" in paragraph 3 ("the reservoir there is spent") refers to

options.A: the molecular structure
options.B: a heavy structural load
options.C: the reversible bond
options.D: a given spot

questions.explanation: The sentence says capsule systems "can heal a given spot only once, because the reservoir there is spent," so "there" refers to that given spot.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about reversible-bond materials?

options.A: They can heal the same location an unlimited number of times.
options.B: They are unable to reform once broken.
options.C: They are cheaper than capsule-based systems.
options.D: They are unsuitable for parts that must bear large loads.

questions.explanation: Because reversible-bond materials are "often too soft for heavy structural loads," they cannot be relied on for parts bearing large loads.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'And because each tiny flaw can trigger this failure, even a well-made part has a hidden weakness.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Location 1
options.B: Location 2
options.C: Location 3
options.D: Location 4

questions.explanation: The sentence elaborates on how a microscopic crack spreads "until the part fails," so it fits at slot 1, immediately after that failure is described and before the invisibility of damage is discussed.

items.passage: Fiber-reinforced polymer composites are materials that combine strong fibers with a surrounding plastic matrix to achieve properties neither component has alone. The fibers, often glass or carbon, carry most of the load, while the matrix binds them together and transfers stress between them. This division of labor explains the composite's remarkable strength-to-weight ratio. A bundle of loose fibers buckles easily, but once locked in a rigid matrix, the same fibers resist bending and share any force applied to the part. The interface between fiber and matrix is therefore decisive. If the bond is weak, the matrix slips past the fibers under load, and the composite delaminates into useless layers. Engineers consequently treat fiber surfaces with chemical coatings that improve adhesion. These coatings let the part absorb energy gradually rather than failing all at once. The directional nature of the fibers, however, brings a drawback. A composite is strongest along the fiber axis and comparatively weak across it, so designers must align the fibers with the expected forces. When loads arrive from unexpected directions, this anisotropy can leave a structure surprisingly vulnerable, and predicting every such load remains difficult.
paragraphs: Fiber-reinforced polymer composites are materials that combine strong fibers with a surrounding plastic matrix to achieve properties neither component has alone. The fibers, often glass or carbon, carry most of the load, while the matrix binds them together and transfers stress between them. This division of labor explains the composite's remarkable strength-to-weight ratio. A bundle of loose fibers buckles easily, but once locked in a rigid matrix, the same fibers resist bending and share any force applied to the part.

The interface between fiber and matrix is therefore decisive. If the bond is weak, the matrix slips past the fibers under load, and the composite delaminates into useless layers. Engineers consequently treat fiber surfaces with chemical coatings that improve adhesion. These coatings let the part absorb energy gradually rather than failing all at once.

The directional nature of the fibers, however, brings a drawback. A composite is strongest along the fiber axis and comparatively weak across it, so designers must align the fibers with the expected forces. When loads arrive from unexpected directions, this anisotropy can leave a structure surprisingly vulnerable, and predicting every such load remains difficult.

questions.stem: The word "decisive" in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: flexible
options.B: crucial
options.C: temporary
options.D: hidden

questions.explanation: The interface is "decisive" because a weak bond causes failure, so it is crucial to the composite's performance.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, what role does the matrix play in the composite?

options.A: It carries most of the load by itself.
options.B: It replaces the fibers under heavy stress.
options.C: It makes the fibers buckle more easily.
options.D: It binds the fibers and transfers stress between them.

questions.explanation: The passage states the matrix "binds them together and transfers stress between them."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 1 about loose, uncombined fibers?

options.A: They are stronger than fibers held in a matrix.
options.B: They have a higher strength-to-weight ratio than composites.
options.C: They are less able to resist bending on their own.
options.D: They transfer stress better without a matrix.

questions.explanation: Since "a bundle of loose fibers buckles easily" but resists bending once locked in a matrix, loose fibers are less able to resist bending alone.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention chemical coatings in paragraph 2?

options.A: To explain how engineers strengthen the bond between fiber and matrix
options.B: To argue that coatings make composites lighter than metals
options.C: To show that the fiber axis determines composite strength
options.D: To describe how loads arrive from unexpected directions

questions.explanation: Coatings are introduced as the engineers' response to weak adhesion: they "improve adhesion" and help the part absorb energy gradually.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true of fiber-reinforced composites EXCEPT

options.A: They are equally strong in every direction.
options.B: They have a remarkable strength-to-weight ratio.
options.C: They can delaminate if the fiber-matrix bond is weak.
options.D: They are strongest along the fiber axis.

questions.explanation: The passage says a composite is "strongest along the fiber axis and comparatively weak across it," so equal strength in every direction is unsupported.

items.passage: Depth perception is the visual ability to judge how far away objects are and to experience the world in three dimensions. Early thinkers treated this skill as a simple matter of geometry, assuming the eye worked like a passive lens that recorded distances directly from light. On this view, seeing depth required no interpretation; the image on the retina supposedly contained distance as a fixed property. This account, however, could not explain why a flat painting can appear deep or why the same retinal image can be read in more than one way. Later researchers corrected the picture by showing that the brain actively combines many separate cues. Some cues come from comparing the slightly different images formed by the two eyes; others, such as the overlapping of near objects and the shrinking of distant ones, work even with a single eye. The brain weighs these signals against past experience to construct a stable sense of space. Modern studies confirm that this construction is learned and adjustable: people can adapt to distorting lenses within days. Such flexibility, while powerful, also makes perception fallible, since the same cues that normally guide us can be deliberately arranged to deceive.
paragraphs: Depth perception is the visual ability to judge how far away objects are and to experience the world in three dimensions. Early thinkers treated this skill as a simple matter of geometry, assuming the eye worked like a passive lens that recorded distances directly from light. On this view, seeing depth required no interpretation; the image on the retina supposedly contained distance as a fixed property.

This account, however, could not explain why a flat painting can appear deep or why the same retinal image can be read in more than one way. Later researchers corrected the picture by showing that the brain actively combines many separate cues. Some cues come from comparing the slightly different images formed by the two eyes; others, such as the overlapping of near objects and the shrinking of distant ones, work even with a single eye.

The brain weighs these signals against past experience to construct a stable sense of space. Modern studies confirm that this construction is learned and adjustable: people can adapt to distorting lenses within days. Such flexibility, while powerful, also makes perception fallible, since the same cues that normally guide us can be deliberately arranged to deceive.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?

options.A: The reasons a flat painting can appear three-dimensional
options.B: The role of the two eyes in comparing retinal images
options.C: How the understanding of depth perception shifted from passive recording to active construction
options.D: The experiments in which people adapt to distorting lenses

questions.explanation: The passage traces a shift from the early "passive lens" view to the modern view that "the brain actively combines many separate cues," which is its overall focus.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, which cue can work with only a single eye?

options.A: Comparing the different images formed by the two eyes
options.B: Recording distance as a fixed retinal property
options.C: Adapting to lenses that distort vision
options.D: The overlapping of near objects and shrinking of distant ones

questions.explanation: The passage states that cues "such as the overlapping of near objects and the shrinking of distant ones, work even with a single eye."

questions.stem: How does paragraph 2 relate to paragraph 1?

options.A: It gives examples that support the view described in paragraph 1.
options.B: It restates paragraph 1 using more technical vocabulary.
options.C: It introduces an entirely unrelated topic.
options.D: It identifies weaknesses in the view from paragraph 1 and presents a revised account.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 opens by noting the earlier account "could not explain" certain facts and then describes how "later researchers corrected the picture," challenging and revising paragraph 1.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 1 about the early thinkers' view?

options.A: They relied on comparing images from both eyes.
options.B: They did not regard the brain as interpreting the retinal image.
options.C: They studied how people adapt to distorting lenses.
options.D: They believed depth could not be seen with one eye.

questions.explanation: Because early thinkers held that "seeing depth required no interpretation," they did not treat the brain as interpreting the retinal image.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 3, what do modern studies confirm about depth perception?

options.A: It is learned and can adjust to distorting lenses.
options.B: It is fixed at birth and cannot change.
options.C: It depends only on the geometry of light.
options.D: It works identically in every observer.

questions.explanation: The passage states that "this construction is learned and adjustable: people can adapt to distorting lenses within days."
